verbatim: [§4] Now the legislator responsible for this deserves censure; for the law which he framed violates the oath of the People and Council. Under the terms of that oath you swear to exile no one, to imprison no one, to put no one to death, without trial.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a standing oath taken by the People and Council which serves as a rule protecting citizens from being exiled, imprisoned, or executed without a trial.

verbatim: [§4] whereas on this present occasion, when the person ostracized is to be cut off from his country for so long, no accusation has been made, no defence allowed, and the voting is secret.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the specific legal mechanism of ostracism where a citizen can be banished for ten years without a formal accusation or the opportunity to provide a defense.

verbatim: [§5] because the judges are not appointed by lot as in courts of law: in the present decision every member of the community has a voice.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies that in cases of ostracism, the resource of political voice/voting is extended to the entire community (the citizen body) rather than a small group of jurors selected by lot.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] ...especially as the laws forbid the same charge to be brought twice against the same defendant, and you have sworn to observe those laws.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a specific legal prohibition against 'double jeopardy' as a standing rule of the Athenian justice system applying to defendants.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] ...he behaved in so profligate a fashion, bringing mistresses, slave and free, into the bridal house, that he drove his wife, who was a decent woman, to present herself before the Archon, as she was legally entitled to do, and divorce him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that a wife is legally entitled to present herself before a magistrate (the Archon) to seek a divorce based on the husband's misconduct.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] In our treaties with other states we make it a condition that no free man shall be imprisoned or placed in durance, and a heavy fine is prescribed as the penalty for so doing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes a formal legal and diplomatic rule protecting free status individuals from imprisonment, backed by financial penalties.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in competing choruses
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] The law allows the ejection of any member whatsoever of a competing chorus who is not of Athenian birth, and it is forbidden to resist any attempt at such ejection.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law defines choral competition as a resource reserved for those of Athenian birth, explicitly allowing the removal of non-citizens.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] And it seems to me only natural that the judges should thus seek favour with Alcibiades, when they could see that Taureas, who had spent so vast a sum, was being subjected to insults, while his rival, who showed such contempt for the law, was all-powerful.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social norm/convention where wealthy individuals who spend large sums on public services (liturgies) expect and often receive professional favor or legal leeway from judges.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] ...after behaving as I have described, he was rewarded with free entertainment in the Prytaneum; and not content with that, he is for ever taking credit for his victory.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that victors in major games (typically elite/noble citizens) are granted the resource of free public maintenance/entertainment in the city's central hall.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§9] Observing that whenever members of the community are more powerful than the magistrates and the laws, it is impossible for an individual to obtain redress from them, he arranged that punishment for their misdeeds should be exacted by the state [through ostracism].
extracted_rules.reasoning: The institution of ostracism is described as a rule specifically targeting citizens who accumulate too much power, resulting in a loss of their right to remain in the city.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§11] [9] ...especially as the laws forbid the same charge to be brought twice against the same defendant, and you have sworn to observe those laws.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a specific legal prohibition against trying a defendant twice for the same offense, which is a protection afforded to those within the Athenian legal system.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§16] [14] ...he behaved in so profligate a fashion... that he drove his wife, who was a decent woman, to present herself before the Archon, as she was legally entitled to do, and divorce him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that a wife is legally entitled to present herself before the Archon to initiate divorce proceedings.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§20] [18] In our treaties with other states we make it a condition that no free man shall be imprisoned or placed in durance, and a heavy fine is prescribed as the penalty for so doing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a legal norm found in treaties and domestic logic where free status grants a specific protection against being put in chains or durance, unlike slaves.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in competing choruses
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§22] [20] The law allows the ejection of any member whatsoever of a competing chorus who is not of Athenian birth, and it is forbidden to resist any attempt at such ejection.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies a law that restricts participation in certain civic-religious competitions (the chorus of boys) to those of Athenian birth, excluding non-citizens.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§24] [22] That is why the young spend their days in the courts instead of in the gymnasia; that is why our old men fight our battles, while our young men make speeches
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author laments that the current societal climate under Alcibiades' influence has led the youth to abandon their traditional resource (the gymnasium) for the law courts.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§24] [22] ...that is why our old men fight our battles, while our young men make speeches— they take Alcibiades as their model
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage suggests a breakdown in the traditional rule where young men are the primary combatants, noting that currently, old men are fighting while young men stay in the city.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§24] [22] ...after recommending that the people of Melos be sold into slavery, he purchased a woman from among the prisoners and has since had a son by her
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text refers to the historical fact of the Melians being sold into slavery following their defeat, indicating that foreigners conquered in war lose their protection from enslavement.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to free entertainment in the Prytaneum
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§33] Alcibiades... after behaving as I have described, he was rewarded with free entertainment in the Prytaneum; and not content with that, he is for ever taking credit for his victory, as though he had not so much brought Athens into disgrace as won her a garland of honour.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text confirms that high-achieving citizens (Olympic victors or those credited with public success) are granted the material resource of state-funded meals/entertainment in the Prytaneum.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§37] Observing that whenever members of the community are more powerful than the magistrates and the laws, it is impossible for an individual to obtain redress from them, he arranged that punishment for their misdeeds should be exacted by the state.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The institution of ostracism is described as a rule allowing the state to remove individuals who exceed the power of the laws, providing the general citizenry with a resource of protection and redress.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to fund public services from personal wealth
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§44] Of my public services I do not intend to speak. I will say only this: the expenditure required of me I meet, not from monies belonging to the state, but from my own pocket. And yet I have in fact gained victories in the contest of physique, in the torch-race, and at the tragic competitions.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the social and legal expectation that wealthy citizens (like the speaker) provide for the state's festivals and competitions (liturgies) using their private funds.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§38] Yet I hardly deserve to be ostracized, gentlemen, if I do not deserve to be put to death; and if I was acquitted when brought to trial, I cannot deserve to be sent into exile when no trial has taken place; nor after vindicating myself so many times in court can I be thought to merit banishment on the same grounds of accusation again.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The speaker invokes a legal norm/rule where a citizen who has been acquitted in court should not face the same accusations via exile (ostracism), representing a right to finality in legal proceedings.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§41] That of the young, upon whom he has brought the disfavour of the people by his insolence, by his emptying of the gymnasia, and by behaviour which his years do not warrant?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies the young as a specific group that has lost access to their traditional resource (the gymnasium) due to the influence/actions of Alcibiades.
